Co_Investment.practical_use: A VC fund investing $10M in a company's Series B may offer LP co-investment rights for an additional $5M directly into the company. LPs typically pay no management fee and reduced or no carry (0–10%) on co-investments versus standard fund terms of 2% and 20%. Large institutional LPs often negotiate co-investment rights as a condition of fund commitment.
Co_Investment.why_it_matters: Co-investments can significantly enhance LP net returns by reducing fee drag on their best investments — concentrating capital in identified winners without the fee overhead of the fund structure. GPs use co-investment rights to win LP commitments and to close larger rounds in top portfolio companies without exceeding fund concentration limits.

SPV.definition: Special Purpose Vehicle — a legal entity, typically an LLC, created for the purpose of pooling capital from multiple investors to make a single investment into a specific company. SPVs allow syndicates of investors to collectively participate in a deal that they could not or would not do individually, appearing as one consolidated entity on the portfolio company's cap table.
SPV.practical_use: Platforms like AngelList and Assure enable GPs to create SPVs for specific deals, pooling $500K–$10M+ from 10–100 investors into a single entity. The SPV GP earns carry (typically 15–20%) on profits from that specific investment. SPVs are increasingly used for access to competitive late-stage rounds.
SPV.why_it_matters: SPVs simplify cap tables by consolidating many individual investors into one entity, making the company more attractive to institutional lead investors who prefer clean, manageable shareholder registers. For investors, SPVs enable participation in deals at check sizes too small for direct inclusion but aggregated to meet company minimums.

Sidecar_Fund.definition: A dedicated investment vehicle that runs alongside a main fund, allowing the GP to deploy additional capital into specific opportunities beyond the main fund's capacity or concentration limits. Sidecar funds typically have similar economics to the main fund but are raised specifically for a defined opportunity set or to capture overflow allocation.
Sidecar_Fund.practical_use: A VC firm with a $100M main fund with 15% concentration limits might create a $20M sidecar fund focused exclusively on follow-on investments in the top 3 performers. Main fund LPs often have rights of first offer on sidecar fund allocation, allowing them to maintain proportional exposure to the best performers.
Sidecar_Fund.why_it_matters: Sidecar funds allow GPs to capture value in breakout companies beyond what the main fund structure permits, aligning GP and LP interests in best-performing assets. Proper LP disclosure, advisory committee approval, and fairness opinions are essential governance requirements to avoid conflicts of interest in sidecar structures.

Fund_of_Funds.definition: An investment fund that invests in a portfolio of other investment funds — VC funds, PE funds, hedge funds — rather than directly in companies or assets. Fund-of-funds provide LPs with diversification across multiple fund managers, strategies, and vintage years, but add an additional layer of fees on top of underlying fund fees.
Fund_of_Funds.practical_use: Fund-of-funds (Hamilton Lane, HarbourVest, Pantheon) allow smaller investors — family offices, smaller pensions — to access top VC funds with high minimum commitments ($10M+) by pooling capital into a single vehicle. FoF managers charge an additional 0.5–1% management fee and 5–10% carry on top of the underlying fund economics.
Fund_of_Funds.why_it_matters: Fund-of-funds add a second layer of fees that significantly reduces net returns to end investors — often 200–300bps annually. The J-curve is also effectively extended because the FoF's returns depend on multiple underlying funds' J-curves. Nevertheless, FoFs provide access, diversification, and manager selection expertise that smaller LPs cannot independently replicate.

Continuation_Fund.definition: A new fund vehicle created by a GP to hold one or more assets from an existing fund that has not been exited, allowing the GP to extend the holding period for high-quality assets while providing liquidity options to existing LPs who wish to exit. Continuation funds are a form of GP-led secondary transaction.
Continuation_Fund.practical_use: A 10-year fund approaching its end with a high-value private company that has not yet IPO'd may create a continuation fund to hold that asset for another 3–5 years. Existing LPs can sell their stake to secondary buyers at a negotiated price or roll their interest into the new fund and wait for a larger exit.
Continuation_Fund.why_it_matters: Continuation funds resolve the tension between fund lifecycle constraints and optimal holding periods for the best assets. They raise inherent conflict-of-interest concerns — the GP effectively sets the transfer price for assets moving from old to new fund. Third-party fairness opinions and LP advisory committee approval are standard governance requirements.

GP_Stake.definition: A direct equity stake in the general partner entity — the management company — of a fund manager, giving the GP stake investor ongoing economics from the GP's management fees and carried interest across current and future funds. GP stake investing has grown into a distinct and sizable asset class over the past decade.
GP_Stake.practical_use: GP stake investors (Goldman Sachs Petershill, Blue Owl Capital, Dyal Capital) buy minority stakes of 10–25% in established fund managers, typically paying 10–15x earnings on management fees and carry. GPs receive liquidity for early employees and partners, capital for co-investments, and institutional resources in exchange.
GP_Stake.why_it_matters: GP stakes allow established fund managers to provide liquidity to founding teams without selling the firm. For GP stake investors, the thesis is a diversified, fee-income-generating portfolio of minority stakes in top managers that is less correlated to public markets than direct fund or company investing. The growing GP stake market has also increased transparency in fund manager economics.

LP.definition: Limited Partner — an investor in a venture capital or private equity fund who contributes capital but has limited liability (capped at their investment amount) and no role in day-to-day fund management decisions. LPs are the capital providers of the fund and receive the majority of fund profits after GP economics are paid.
LP.practical_use: Typical LPs include university endowments (Harvard, Yale), pension funds (CalPERS, CPPIB), sovereign wealth funds (GIC, Temasek), family offices, foundations, insurance companies, and high-net-worth individuals. LP commitments are legally binding pledges to contribute capital when the GP issues capital calls over the fund's investment period.
LP.why_it_matters: LP relationships are the foundation of the venture capital ecosystem — GPs must deliver sufficient net returns to LPs to raise subsequent funds. LP satisfaction affects a GP's ability to raise capital, access deal flow, and maintain their franchise. The limited partnership agreement governs all aspects of the fund relationship including economics, governance, and reporting obligations.

GP.definition: General Partner — the fund manager responsible for making investment decisions, managing portfolio companies, and returning capital to LPs. The GP has unlimited liability for fund obligations (in theory, though typically structured through entities to limit personal liability), earns management fees for operations, and receives carried interest on profits above the hurdle rate.
GP.practical_use: A GP entity manages the fund on behalf of LPs, making all investment and portfolio management decisions. The GP team includes investment partners who source and lead deals, operating partners who support portfolio companies, and back-office staff managing administration, legal, and fund finance. GPs typically commit 1–2% of fund size as GP commitment to align their own capital with LP interests.
GP.why_it_matters: The GP is the architect of fund strategy, investment thesis, portfolio construction, and LP relations. GP quality is the single most important determinant of fund returns. Evaluating GP quality — track record, team stability, sourcing advantages, portfolio support capabilities, and governance track record — is the primary function of institutional LP investors conducting manager due diligence.
